narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score and a high family income background, you've put together a strong profile in several areas, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 and your choice to pursue part-time studies, which is less common among applicants. However, the university's admission model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 41.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This prediction stemmed primarily from your academic performance in school, where your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile compared to peers, making it the single strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in the bottom decile well below the typical student's standing, reinforced this concern as the third-most influential element, suggesting early struggles that carried forward. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 38—above the average of 36.7—strongly pulled the prediction in the opposite direction, toward expecting you to pass, acting as the second-most important factor in your favor. While your high family income and part-time status didn't sway the outcome as much, they fit a profile where external supports exist but haven't yet translated to the consistent academic decile rankings the model prioritizes for bar success. Compared to typical students, who land around the middle of their class in both early and later years, your lower deciles stand out as the key deviation driving this decision, even with your stronger LSAT. The model weighs these patterns heavily because they've historically separated those who pass the bar on the first try from those who don't. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, as even modest improvements there could flip future predictions. You might also seek targeted LSAT-level prep to amplify your strengths or explore schools with more holistic reviews beyond predictive models. Retaking the LSAT isn't urgent given your solid score, but documenting any upward trends in your academics would help. With your background, persistence could easily shift the odds—many in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps. Keep building on what worked for your LSAT, and you'll likely see better results ahead.
